experiment: F-EVAL4 hardening: continuous scoring + session-type stratification
hypothesis: Continuous scoring (replacing cliff-edge thresholds) produces 2+ different rating changes vs baseline. DOMEX sessions show higher avg_lp than non-DOMEX.

method.intervention_1: Added _continuous_score() interpolation function mapping metrics to 0.0-3.0 continuous range across all 4 goal scorers
method.intervention_2: Added session-type stratification in _load_session_log() tagging sessions as DOMEX/non-DOMEX from SESSION-LOG descriptions
method.intervention_3: Changed next_improvement_target selection from discrete to continuous scores for precision
method.baseline: eval_sufficiency.py at S417 (window=min(50,available), session-count floor n<5)

session_stratification.finding: avg_lp is DOMEX-dominated (18/19 sessions). Non-DOMEX barely represented in window.

margin_analysis.Protect_to_SUFFICIENT: drift must drop from 8.3% to <6.0% AND maintain drop_rate>0

verdict_rationale: Discrete verdicts unchanged (0 rating changes, expected 2+). But continuous scoring correctly differentiates previously-tied goals (Protect 1.71 < Increase 1.84), changing the improvement target. Session stratification confirms DOMEX dominance (94.7% of sessions). Hardening value is in precision, not verdict change.
diff_from_expectation: Expected 2+ discrete rating changes — actual 0. Continuous scoring adds diagnostic precision without changing discrete outcomes at current metric values. The cliff-edge problem manifests only when metrics are near thresholds; current values are well within their discrete bands.
prescriptions: Protect is the true binding constraint (proxy_k_drift 8.3% > 6% target). Compaction should be prioritized over production.
Non-DOMEX session representation too low (n=1 in 50-session window) for meaningful stratification. Need more maintenance-only session data.
Continuous composite (74%) is a more honest overall metric than discrete (58%) — consider using it in orient.py.
